hero_summary: Ajam Ushayran is a maqam of the Ajam family. Its tonic is Ajam Ashiran (low B-flat), and in classical Turkish theory it is presented as the transposition of Jaharkah to that degree, with a basically descending sayr.
short_identity: A maqam of the Ajam family whose tonic is Ajam Ashiran (low B-flat). In practical Arabic explanation it may be understood as Ajam on Ajam Ashiran, but the more precise Turkish description treats it as a transposed Jaharkah with a descending sayr.

quick_facts.lower_jins: Jins Ajam on Ajam Ashiran (low B-flat tonic)
quick_facts.upper_jins: A connected Ajam/Jaharkah continuation in the upper region
quick_facts.character: A clear Ajam/Jaharkah color with a low tonic and a descending sayr
quick_facts.common_start: It tends to center first in the upper or middle region, then descend to confirm Ajam Ashiran as the final tonic

sayr.summary: Ajam Ushayran is a maqam of the Ajam family, and in classical Turkish theory it is presented as the transposition of Jaharkah to the degree of Ajam Ashiran (low B-flat tonic). That means its structural logic is the logic of an explicit major/Jaharkah framework, but with a lower tonic on Ajam Ashiran and a basically descending sayr. For that reason, it is not merely “Ajam on another note” in a simplified sense, but a maqam with a stable theoretical profile in terms of tonic, strong tone, sayr, and temporary cadential centers.
resting_tones: Ajam Ashiran (low B-flat tonic): the final tonic of the maqam
Ajam (middle B-flat): the strong tone or ghammaz, where an important secondary stabilizing center appears
Husayni Ashiran (low E): the leading tone toward the tonic, functioning as the yeden of the maqam
motion_notes: Its sayr is basically descending in the classical Turkish description
It is not accurate to explain it only as a major scale on a low B-flat without mentioning that its original sayr logic is descending
Its identity is not reducible to its major color alone, but also depends on the points of emphasis, the role of Ajam as the strong tone, and Husayni Ashiran as the leading tone toward the cadence
It may sound close to the Western major scale to an untrained ear, but its maqamic personality is more specific than that
common_path: Center first in the upper or middle region
Bring out the clear Ajam/Jaharkah material
Show Husayni Ashiran as the leading approach to the cadence
Close fully on Ajam Ashiran (low B-flat tonic)

common_modulations: It belongs to the Ajam family and shares the broad major color with Ajam, but differs from Maqam Ajam mainly in the position of the tonic, since Ajam Ushayran resolves on Ajam Ashiran (low B-flat)
Its relation to Jaharkah is very close, because the classical Turkish description treats it as the transposition of the Jaharkah sequence to Ajam Ashiran

It may be confused with Rast by the non-specialist because of the clarity of its tonal stability, but Rast depends on a different logic of ajnas and the third degree, whereas Ajam Ushayran presents a clearly Ajam/Jaharkah organization

There is no major school dispute over the basic identity of the maqam; what differs is mainly the mode of explanation between the more precise Turkish structural school and the shorter Arabic practical explanation
